segments.text: Chapter 5 Proteins: primary structure

5.1 What Architectural Arrangements Characterize Protein Structure?
Proteins are classed according to shape and and solubility
Shape - globular or fibrous
The four levels of protein structure are:
- Primary (1 ° ) - sequence
- Secondary (2 ° ) - local structures - H-bonds
- Tertiary (3 ° ) - overall 3-dimensional shape
- Quaternary (4 ° ) - subunit organization

Primary structure of proteins
Proteins are unbranched chains of amino acids
Amino acids are joined together through an amide bond
The amide bond is called a peptide bond .
Short stretches of amino acids are known as peptides
2 amino acids: dipeptide
Up to ~ 20-40 amino acids (oligopeptides)
Larger: polypeptide

Peptides
Peptides and proteins are formed when amino acids are joined together by amide (peptide) bonds.
A dipeptide is two amino acids joined together by one amide bond.
The amide bond is called a peptide bond .

Peptides
A tripeptide has three amino acids joined together by one amide bond.
Proteins are large polypeptides

Peptides
The amino acids Ala and Ser can combine in this way:
Peptide bond formation: dehydration reaction
Breaking peptide bond: hydrolysis

Peptides
Or, the two can combine with Ser first and Ala second:

Peptides
The amino acid with the free –NH 3 + group is the N-terminal amino acid and is written on the left .
The amino acid with the free –COO − group is the C-terminal amino acid and is written on the right .
Protein sequence is always written from N- to C- terminus

Peptides
Draw a Dipeptide from Two Amino Acids
Identify the N- and C-termini

Peptides

Example: Neuropeptides—Enkephalins and Pain Relief
One of the enkephalins, met-enkephalin :
How many amino acids are there is met-enkaphalin?
5 amino acids
Chapter 6 Proteins: Secondary, Tertiary, and Quaternary Structure

Secondary Structure
The secondary structure is the arrangement of localized regions of a protein.
These regions arise due to hydrogen bonding between the N—H group of one amide with the C═O group of another.
Two stable arrangements are the a -helix and the b -pleated sheet .
Most proteins have regions of a -helix and b -pleated sheet, and other regions that are random arrangements.

Secondary Structure
The a -helix:
H-bonds: along the axis of the helix

Alpha helix
Stabilized by main chain H-bonds  (NH and C=O)
Typically right handed with 3.6 amino acids per turn
Note: Side chains are NOT involved in helix H-bonding
Side chains point outward

Secondary Structure
The b -pleated sheet:
H-bonds: across the sheet, between different strands of the sheet
β-strand
β-strand
Multiple strands form one sheet

Beta sheets
Beta sheets can be parallel or anti-parralel
N to C line up, or alternate
Side chains point above and below the plane in an alternating fashion

Secondary Structure
Shorthand symbols on a protein ribbon diagram:
ribbon diagrams simplify the way we view protein structure

Proteins B. Secondary Structure
ribbon diagrams simplify the way we view protein structure

Why are secondary structures favored?
Even through peptide chains are flexible, bond rotation can be limited
There is a limited amount of “favorable” arrangements due to
Stabilization by intra-chain interactions (mainly H-bonds)
Destabilization by steric clashes
Limited rotation around the peptide bond

Phi and Psi angles
Dihedral angles play an important part in determining protein structure
Phi and Psi angles provide rotational degrees of freedom to the peptide chain
Omega is the angle around the peptide bond (constant ~ 180)
Phi: N-C α , Psi: C α -C

Phi and Psi angles
Dihedral angles range from -180 to 180 degrees
Example: Phi at 180 and at 45 degrees
Combinations of phi and psi angles give rise to specific protein structure elements

The Ramachandran plot
A plot of psi vs. phi value
Shows allowable combinations for secondary structures
Beta sheets
Alpha helices
Left handed helices (rare)

Ramachandran plot: Gly & Pro
Glycine and Proline are important in protein structure
Glycine
Proline
Other
Glycine: provides structural flexibility in proteins
Proline: provides structural rigidity in proteins
Both can be “helix breakers”
